coredata.dc:description: Highly sensitive monitoring of pesticides has garnered attention for food safety. Here, a ratiometric fluorescent probe (AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs) was fabricated for specific and sensitive sensing of glyphosate. Surface modifications revealed the chelation-enhanced fluorescence process between the surface -OH of AG-CDs and Al3+. Positively charged green-emission AG-CDs-Al3+ assembled with negatively charged red-emission CdTe QDs, and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) occurred from CdTe QDs to AG CDs-Al3+. Glyphosate specifically coordinated with Al3+, causing remarkable variations in F500/F630 (linear range, 0.2–4.0 μM; detection limit, 5.7 nM) and a distinguishable green-yellow-red color change on the paper platform (linear range, 0.5–8.0 μM; detection limit, 26 nM). Furthermore, for real sample analysis, the sensor has the advantages of fast response (5 min), excellent accuracy (spiked recoveries, 95.0–105.0 %), high precision (relative standard deviation, < 3.33 %), and superior specificity, which endowed the designed sensing platform with great potential practicality in the field of food analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly sensitive monitoring of pesticides has garnered attention for food safety. Here, a ratiometric fluorescent probe (AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs) was fabricated for specific and sensitive sensing of glyphosate. Surface modifications revealed the chelation-enhanced fluorescence process between the surface -OH of AG-CDs and Al3+. Positively charged green-emission AG-CDs-Al3+ assembled with negatively charged red-emission CdTe QDs, and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) occurred from CdTe QDs to AG CDs-Al3+. Glyphosate specifically coordinated with Al3+, causing remarkable variations in F500/F630 (linear range, 0.2–4.0 μM; detection limit, 5.7 nM) and a distinguishable green-yellow-red color change on the paper platform (linear range, 0.5–8.0 μM; detection limit, 26 nM). Furthermore, for real sample analysis, the sensor has the advantages of fast response (5 min), excellent accuracy (spiked recoveries, 95.0–105.0 %), high precision (relative standard deviation, < 3.33 %), and superior specificity, which endowed the designed sensing platform with great potential practicality in the field of food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate, a low-cost, high-efficiency, and broad-spectrum herbicide known as N-methylglycine phosphate, is widely used in economic crops for weeds control (Deng, Guo, et al., 2024; Deng, Hu, et al., 2024). Due to the long-term massive use, residues of glyphosate in water and farm products can accumulate in the human body through the food chain, posing potential threats to human health, such as reproductive toxicity, teratogenic effect, and mutagenic property (Li et al., 2024; Mazuryk et al., 2024). Since 2015, glyphosate has been classified as a Class 2 A probable carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Martinek et al., 2024). In this regard, the Chinese Ministry of Health has established maximum residue levels for glyphosate at 0.5, 3.0, and 5.0 mg/kg in fruits, vegetables, and grains (Che et al., 2024; Deng, Hu, et al., 2024; He et al., 2024). Thereafter, fabricating reliable, effective, and sensitive sensing strategies for glyphosate detection is becoming increasingly significant and urgent for food safety and environmental protection.

To date, methods for glyphosate detection focus on chromatography, fluorescence, colorimetric, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical, and chemiluminescence approaches (Chang et al., 2023; He et al., 2024; Ren et al., 2024; Valle et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2023). Among these methods, fluorescence methods come out in an unending flow due to the advantages of superior sensitivity, technical simplicity, excellent reproducibility, rapid response, and convenient on-site visual detection (Ren et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2024). The classic case relies on the irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity by glyphosate, and then the reduced production of thiocholine can be used as the intermediate to directly/indirectly trigger the fluorescent signal response (Rasheed, 2023). The AChE-based sensing strategies have obtained substantial achievements, however, lack of sufficient sensitivity and specificity, susceptible to environmental factors, and inactivation of AChE remain challenges (Tong et al., 2023; Xiao et al., 2024). Notably, -COOH, imino, and phosphonyl groups of glyphosate can chelate with metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Tb3+, Zr4+). Based on this property, several fluorescent methods using organic small-molecular dyes (Che et al., 2024), carbon dots (CDs) (Yang et al., 2024), metal/covalent organic frameworks (Zhu et al., 2024), and metal nanoclusters (Ma et al., 2023) as sensors have been designed for AChE-free detection of glyphosate (Table S1). More importantly, by comparison with single-signal output modes, ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategies offer inherent self-calibration capability, mitigate or even eliminate interference from various target-independent factors, and thus enhance detection accuracy and reliability (Alhazzani et al., 2025; Cao et al., 2025; Tong et al., 2023). It is worth noting that dual fluorescence emissions with reverse changing trends can distinctly amplify signal responses with improved sensitivity and facilitate vivid changes in color tonality for point-of-care quantification (Chen et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024). Zhang et al. designed a ratiometric fluorescent system based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer between blue-emitting CDs and yellow-emitting 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP, o-phenylenediamine oxidized by Cu2+), while the chelation between glyphosate and Cu2+ weakened the DAP emission (570 nm) and enhanced the CDs emission (470 nm) (Zhang et al., 2024). Deng et al. fabricated a ratiometric fluorescent sensor by mixing red-emitting gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and blue-emitting CDs, and in the presence of glyphosate, CDs emission was quenched by dynamic quenching effect and AuNCs emission was strengthened by aggregation-induced enhancement effect, which displayed sensitive color changes from blue to red (Deng, Guo, et al., 2024). Obviously, interaction between fluorescent building blocks and glyphosate is crucial for designing ratiometric fluorescent sensors with reverse signal response. So far, few works have been reported. Consequently, it has broad application prospects to design a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for sensitive, selective, accurate, precise, and rapid detection and quantification of glyphosate in food samples.

Among fluorescent probes, CDs and CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) exhibit simple synthetic procedures, good water solubility, attractive optical and electronic characteristics, and facile functionalization. To further take full use of these unique features, CDs/CdTe QDs are considered to facilitate the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensing sensors (Fan et al., 2023; Miao et al., 2024). Wu et al. selected blue-emitting CDs as an unchanged reference signal and red-emitting CdTe QDs as a reaction unit to respond to tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone pesticide by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and dynamic quenching mechanisms (Wu et al., 2023). Qin et al. integrated green-emitting CDs and red-emitting CdTe QDs for the quantitative detection of methyl parathion, where internal filter effect between CDs and p-nitrophenol (hydrolyzed product of methyl parathion) quenched the green fluorescence, and the red fluorescence remained stable (Qin et al., 2023). Chen et al. established a Förster resonance energy transfer system based on CDs (Em, 445 nm) and thioglycolic acid-capped CdTe QDs (Em, 593 nm). After adding mesosulfuron-methyl, fluorescent intensities of both emissions decreased via PET and static quenching effects (Chen, Li, et al., 2024; Chen, Liu, et al., 2024). To the best of our knowledge, CDs/CdTe QDs-based ratiometric fluorescent systems for pesticides sensing are rarely reported, not to mention dual-reverse-signal modes (Chen et al., 2023). Thus, designing strategies for CDs/CdTe QDs-based dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent methods still have much room to fabricate to meet the demands of extremely sensitive pesticide detection.

In this work, we have, for the first time, developed a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent strategy based on Al3+ chelation triggering PET between AG-CDs-Al3+ and CdTe QDs for ultrasensitive sensing of glyphosate (Scheme 1 ). Green-emitting CDs (AG-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of ascorbic acid and glycerol were screened out, and surface structure tuning revealed that surface -OH groups specifically chelated with Al3+ to form AG-CDs-Al3+ with efficiently enhanced fluorescence due to the chelation enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. Meanwhile, positively charged AG-CDs-Al3+ adsorbed negatively charged CdTe QDs through electrostatic interaction force, and PET from red-emitting CdTe QDs to green-emitting AG-CDs-Al3+ effectively enhanced the green emission and quenched the red emission. Furthermore, based on the competitive interaction of Al3+ with AG-CDs and glyphosate, the disassembly of AG-CDs-Al3+ weakened the CHEF and PET processes, which largely decreased the green emission and recovered the red emission, resulting in distinct color change of a green-yellow-red evolution. The reverse variation of dual fluorescence signals from the synergistic effect of CHEF and PET achieved signal amplification and ultrasensitive quantification of glyphosate. The fabricated approach was further applied to detect glyphosate in food samples, demonstrating its potential for practical applications in food safety monitoring.

Ethylene glycol (99 %, AR), glycerol (99 %, AR), meso-erythritol (99 %, AR), ethanol (99 %, AR), ascorbic acid (99 %, AR), NaCl (99 %, AR), tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate (TBAPF6, 98 %), BaSO4 (99 %, AR), and quinine sulfate (99 %, BR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Red-emitting mercaptopropionic acid-capped CdTe QDs (240.01 g/mol) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). MnCl2·4H2O (99 %, AR), KCl (99 %, AR), LiCl (99 %, AR), FeCl3·6H2O (97 %, AR), CaCl2 (96 %, AR), BaCl2 (99 %, AR), Ni(NO3)2 (99 %, AR), ZnSO4·7H2O (99 %, AR), Cd(NO3)2·4H2O (98 %, AR), MgSO4·7H2O (98 %, AR), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (99 %, AR), Hg(NO3)2 (99 %, AR), FeCl2 (98 %, AR), Na2SO4 (99 %, GR), NaNO3 (99 %, AR), Na3PO4 (96 %, GR), Na2CO3 (99.5 %, AR), NaNO2 (99.99 %, GR), HCl (36–38 %, GR), H2SO4 (95.0–98.0 %, GR), NaOH (98 %, AR), NaBH4 (99 %, AR), 1,3-propane sulfonate (PS, 99 %, GC), triethylamine (99.5 %, GC), and 1,4-dioxane (99.5 %, GC) were gained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Carbaryl (99 %, AR), thiophanate-methyl (TM, 99 %, AR), and parathion-methyl (MP, 1 mg/mL, AR) were provided by ANPEL Laboratory Technologies (Shanghai, China). Fipronil (95 %, AR), naled (95 %, AR), dipterex (99 %, AR), dichlorvos (1 mg/mL, AR), thiram (99 %, AR), glyphosate (98 %, AR), parathion (1 mg/mL, AR), and carbendazim (98 %, AR) were brought from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). DL-Alanine (Ala, 98.5 %, BR), L-isoleucine (L-Ile, 99 %, BR), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe, 99 %, AR), L-leucine (L-Leu, 99 %, AR), l-serine (L-Ser, 99 %, BR) were gained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 0.22 μm filtration membrane was provided by Cinjinghua Co. (Shanghai, China). Whatman 4 filter paper was obtained from GE Healthcare Life Sciences (Beijing, China). The ultrapure water used in all experiments (18.25 MΩ cm) was purified using an ultrapure water system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Morphologies and sizes of CDs were performed on an HT7700 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi, Japan) at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with Escalab Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on Empyrean (PANalytical, Netherlands). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) and fluorescent spectra were performed by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA), respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded by Spectrum Two (PerkinElmer Ltd., USA). Fluorescent decay time curves were collected on a Fluo Time 100 spectrofluorometer (PicoQuant, Germany). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were performed on a CHI660E Electrochemical analyzer (Shanghai CH Instrument Company, China). pH values were measured by a Five Easy plus pH Meter (Mettler Toledo, China). Z-potential values were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano series equipment (Malvern, England). UV − vis diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 (Persee, China) UV − vis spectrometer in the range of 300–800 nm with BaSO4 as a reference. Portable UV lamp (365 nm, 16 W) was bought from Gongyi Yuhua Instrument Co., Ltd. (Gongyi, China).

Ascorbic acid (0.6 g) was ultrasonically dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL), and then ethylene glycol (7.5 mL, 0.137 mol), glycerol (10 mL, 0.137 mol), or meso-erythritol (16.73 g, 0.137 mol) was added under continuous magnetic stirring. Mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 20 min and then heated in a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) at 160 °C for 80 min. After cooling to room temperature, solutions were centrifuged, while supernatants were dialyzed (500 Da) over 48 h to eliminate unreacted precursors, filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, and freeze-dried to yield AE-CDs, AG-CDs, and AM-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1A).

Chemical passivation of surface oxygen-containing groups of CDs was performed according to previously reported with minor modifications (Gao et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2025). 1,3-Propane sulfonate (PS) was selected to react with -COOH and -OH to form ester and ether to prepare CDs-PS with only CO. NaBH4 was used to reduce CO into -OH group to synthesize CDs-NaBH4 with -OH and -COOH. NaOH can hydrolyze ester to release -COOH, and corresponding CDs-PS-NaOH and CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH were synthesized (Fig. 1A).

Synthesis of CDs-PS: CDs solution (10 mg, 2 mL) was mixed with 1,4-dioxane (20 mL), PS (2.0 g), and triethylamine (2 mL), and then stirred at 40 °C for 24 h. Resulting CDs-PS solution was evaporated to remove solvent, redispersed in water and dialyzed with NaCl (0.1 M) solution for 24 h and water for 72 h.

Synthesis of CDs-PS-NaOH: CDs-PS (5 mg) was added into NaOH solution (0.5 M, 5 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Resulting CDs-PS-NaOH solution was neutralized using HCl and then dialyzed (500 Da) with water for 72 h.

Synthesis of CDs-NaBH4: CDs (20 mg) were mixed with NaBH4 solution (0.5 M, 50 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Resulting CDs-NaBH4 solution was neutralized with HCl and then dialyzed (500 Da) with water for 72 h.
Synthesis of CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH: Using CDs-NaBH4 (5 mg) as starting materials, the synthesis procedures for CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH were the same with those mentioned above for CDs-PS-NaOH.
After being dialyzed, CDs-PS, CDs-PS-NaOH, CDs-NaBH4, and CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and free-dried.

CV measurements were achieved at room temperature with Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, glassy carbon electrode deposited with AG-CDs, AG-CDs-Al3+, or CdTe QDs (3 mm in diameter) as the working electrode, and acetonitrile including TBAPF6 (0.1 M) as the solution.

AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs were fabricated by the self-assembly of AG-CDs, Al3+, and CdTe QDs. Briefly, AG-CDs (5 mL, 0.3 mg/mL), Al3+ (5 mL, 30 μM), and CdTe QDs solution (5 mL, 0.15 mM) were mixed and ultrasonically treated at 25 °C for 5 min. For glyphosate detection, AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs solutions with different concentrations of glyphosate were incubated at 25 °C for 5 min. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer under 400 nm excitation. Calibration equation between fluorescence intensity ratio values of F500/F630 and concentrations of glyphosate were plotted for quantification.

Round pieces of non-fluorescent Waterman 4 filter paper (diameter 8.0 mm) were prepared by a handcrafted puncher, which was then soaked in AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs solution for 10 min by automatic depositing the ratiometric fluorescent probe, taken out, and naturally dried at room temperature. Then, different concentrations of glyphosate solution (5 μL) were dropped onto the treated filter papers and incubated at 25 °C for 5 min. Subsequently, the filter papers were placed under a self-made portable device including smartphone (XIAOMI 14, Xiaomi Corporation), a 365 nm UV lamp, and a dark box (Yang et al., 2024), and the fluorescent images were photographed. Finally, RGB (red, green, and blue channels) values for fluorescent images were collected using the “Color Grab” APP installed in the smartphone. The G/R ratios of fluorescent images were calculated for visual quantitative analysis of glyphosate. All tests were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three food samples (i.e., pear, cantaloupe, turnip) were bought from a local supermarket (Changsha, China) and used to evaluate the analytical reliability and application potentials of the developed dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent strategy. Each food sample (10.0 g) was sprayed with different amounts of glyphosate. The spiked sample was ultrasonically soaked with deionized water (10 mL) for 10 min, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane for further analysis using the proposed method. Each analysis was operated in triplicate (n = 3), and results were presented as mean ± standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and the chelation with Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid and polyols (i.e., ethylene glycol, glycerol, meso-erythritol) were selected as precursors in the hydrothermal synthesis of three CDs, designated as AE-CDs, AG-CDs, and AM-CDs, respectively (Fig. 1A). TEM images in Fig. 1B–D indicate that AE-CDs, AG-CDs and AM-CDs are uniformly dispersed spherical structures with similar average particle sizes at 6.45 ± 1.95, 7.85 ± 1.57 and 7.28 ± 1.22 nm. High resolution TEM images (insets in Fig. 1B–D) display high crystallinity with a surface spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite for all three CDs (Tong et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024). XRD patterns (Fig. S1A) display a broad peak at 22.1o, 23.2o, and 23.3o for AE-CDs, AG-CDs and AM-CDs, respectively, indicating sp2 hybridization within the amorphous core (Sajwan et al., 2024). FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1E) feature absorption bands at 3283, 2970/2920, 1645, and 1033 cm−1 for stretching vibrations of OH, CH, C=C/C=O, and C–O–C groups and 1407 cm−1 for bending vibration of CH group, indicating the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized conjugated structures and the abundance of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs (Liu et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024). XPS results further confirm the chemical compositions and surface functional groups. The XPS full scan spectra exhibit two characteristic peaks at about 285.8 and 533.1 eV for C and O elements with different relative elemental contents, such as 59.07 % and 40.93 % for AE-CDs, 66.97 % and 33.03 % for AG-CDs, 71.82 % and 28.18 % for AM-CDs (Fig. 1F). High-resolution XPS spectra for O1s and C1s (Fig. 1G and S1B) identify peaks for C–OH, O–C=O, CO, and CC bonds (Tong et al., 2023), while the highest content for C–OH group is observed for AG-CDs (39.42 %), followed by AM-CDs (33.69 %) and AE-CDs (18.69 %). The XPS findings are consistent with the FT-IR results, demonstrating a clear correlation between the functional group contents and the chosen precursors. Optical characteristics of AE-CDs, AG-CDs, and AM-CDs are presented in Fig. S2. The strong absorption bands observed at 267, 247, and 285 nm for AE-CDs, AG-CDs, and AM-CDs are attributed to the π–π* transitions within the conjugated systems. Fluorescence excitation spectra of the CDs reveal that the emission at 540 nm, which is independent of excitation wavelength, primarily originates from the n–π* transition absorption band.

The surface state of CDs has been reported to significantly affect their UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra (Yang et al., 2023). Al3+ as a hard acid can be spontaneously and strongly chelate with oxygen-containing groups of CDs (hard bases) to realize specific sensing (Liu et al., 2023). When Al3+ was introduced into AG-CDs, AM-CDs, or AE-CDs solution, the absorption values at a longer wavelength (about 410 nm) enhance obviously (Fig. 2A), indicating the formation of ground state complex (Yang et al., 2023). Furthermore, FT-IR spectra for CDs-Al3+ system (Fig. S3A) indicate the appearance of AlO bond at about 825 cm−1 (Zhang et al., 2023), and the OH stretching vibration blue-shifts to 3230 cm − 1. Further, the XPS full scan spectrum of AG-CDs-Al3+ exhibits a typical peak for Al (Fig. S3B), and AlO absorption peaks at binding energies of 74.90 eV (Al 2p3/2) and 74.27 eV (Al 2p1/2) present in the Al 2p spectrum (Fig. S3C). Consequently, a new complex may form due to the strong chelation ability between surface -OH and Al3+. To elucidate the specific oxygen-containing functional groups involved in chelation with Al3+ and to understand the structure-property relationships during Al3+ sensing, a selective deactivation strategy has been devised to modulate the content of surface functional groups (Fig. 1A). FT-IR spectra and detailed characterization in Fig. S4 illustrate the successful synthesis of CDs-PS with -COOH and -OH groups passivated (existence of CO), CDs-PS-NaOH with -OH group passivated (existence of -COOH and CO), CDs-NaBH4 with CO reduced into -OH group (existence of -OH and -COOH), and CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH with the presence of -COOH. The contribution of functional groups to Al3+ sensing can be differentiated by comparing the change of UV–vis and fluorescent spectra among CDs, CDs-PS, CDs-PS-NaOH, CDs-NaBH4, and CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH. UV–vis spectra in Fig. 2B clearly show the increased absorbance at about 410 nm for CDs-NaBH4 with the introduction of Al3+, similar phenomena with those for CDs. Whereas for CDs-PS, CDs-PS-NaOH, and CDs-NaBH4-PS-NaOH, -OH group of them has been passivated and UV–vis spectra before and after addition of Al3+ present negligible change (Fig. S5). Furthermore, AG-CDs, AM-CDs, and AE-CDs demonstrate a highly selective response to Al3+ (Fig. S6), and the fluorescence intensities of AG-CDs, AM-CDs, and AE-CDs at 500 nm gradually increase along with the increase of Al3+ concentration (Fig. S7). As discovered in Fig. 2C and S8, only CDs-NaBH4 exhibits the fluorescence turn-on response to Al3+. These findings concluded that surface -OH rather than -COOH and CO chelates with Al3+ to form CDs-Al3+ complex for enhanced absorption and fluorescence. Consequently, AG-CDs with the highest C–OH content present the best sensitivity to Al3+ response (Fig. 1G and S7DF), which is utilized as a model for further investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensor and mechanism discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing is based on the target-induced variation of fluorescent intensities from two or more different emission peaks, which can improve the accuracy and sensitivity due to the self-correction capability and benefit visual quantification due to the wide color variation range (Chen et al., 2023). Furthermore, dual-reverse-signal type fluorescent sensor generates two completely opposite fluorescent changes, which amplify the signal responses and thus largely improve the detection sensitivity (Yang et al., 2024). It is noted that investigating sensing mechanism is crucial for the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

The fluorescence enhancement mechanism of AG-CDs by Al3+ was first investigated. The time-resolved fluorescent decay behaviors and quantum yields (QY) of AG-CDs and the AG-CDs-Al3+ complex were studied. With the addition of Al3+, the calculated average fluorescence lifetime increases significantly from 17.72 ns to 23.92 ns (Fig. 2D), and the QY enhances from 14.33 % to 24.62 % (quinine sulfate as the reference). Accordingly, the radiative rate constant (k r) and the non-radiative rate constant (k nr) were calculated (Long et al., 2019), which concludes that k r for AG-CDs-Al3+ increases from 0.81 to 1.03 ns−1 and k nr for AG-CDs-Al3+ decreases from 4.83 to 3.5 ns−1. Thus, the AG-CDs-Al3+ complex with increased k r value presents higher fluorescent efficiency. Additionally, as showcased in Fig. 2E, zeta potential investigation indicates that AG-CDs exhibit a significantly negative surface charge (−19.52 mV) due to the presence of negatively charged species (e.g., -OH and -COOH). Meanwhile, after chelation between -OH and Al3+, AG-CDs-Al3+ complex is positively charged (6.61 mV) (Fig. 2E). Furthermore, a fluorometric titration was performed. The binding constant (K) of Al3+ with AG-CDs was determined using the modified Benesi-Hildebrand equation: 1/ΔF = 1/ΔFmax + (1/K[C]) (1/ΔFmax). Here, ΔF = F − F0 and ΔFmax = Fmax − F0, where F0, F, and Fmax represent the fluorescence intensities of AG-CDs in the absence of Al3+, with the existence of different concentrations of Al3+, and at full saturation, respectively. K is the binding constant, and [C] is the Al3+ concentration. As presented in Fig. S9A, the K value can be found to be 6.56 × 104 M−1, indicating the relatively strong affinity between the AG-CDs and Al3+. In addition, the E g values of AG-CDs and AG-CDs-Al3+ were found to be 1.87 and 2.11 eV (Fig. S9B,C). Then based on voltammograms (Fig. S9D,E), the HOMO and LUMO values of AG-CDs were calculated to be −4.40 and − 6.27 eV, and those for AG-CDs-Al3+ were − 4.71 and − 6.82 eV (Fig. S9F). The decreased E HOMO value of AG-CDs-Al3+ also indicates the formation of stable complex. All the phenomena testify the strong chelation between AG-CDs and Al3+ results in the enhanced fluorescence, convincing enough to support the CHEF mechanism (Zhou et al., 2024).

We then selected negatively charged red-emitting CdTe QDs to combine with AG-CDs-Al3+ via electrostatic gravitational forces to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe. CdTe QDs were characterized and verified by TEM, FT-IR, XRD, and XPS (Fig. S10) (Guan et al., 2024). Zeta potential of AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs probe is 0.98 mV (Fig. 2E), attributed to electrostatic neutralization. In the UV–vis spectra, the characteristic absorption peak of AG-CDs-Al3+ at 257 nm blue-shifts to 247 nm after the addition of CdTe QDs (Fig. S11), which indicates the successful formation of fluorescent nano-assemblies. Red-emitting CdTe QDs exhibit two excitation peaks at 280 and 320 nm, and an emission peak at 630 nm with an excitation-independent behavior (Fig. S12A,B). Different concentrations of Al3+ or AG-CDs have no effect on the fluorescent intensity of CdTe QDs (Fig. S12C,D) and the addition of Al3+ or AG-CDs seldom affects the UV–vis spectrum of CdTe QDs (Fig. S12E), revealing that CdTe QDs cannot form complexes with Al3+ or AG-CDs and there is no electron or energy transfer between AG-CDs and CdTe QDs. Interestingly, by mixing AG-CDs-Al3+ with CdTe QDs, the emission peak at 500 nm for AG-CDs-Al3+ enhances obviously while the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs at 630 nm is quenched significantly, and the quenching efficiency reaches 77.6 % when the concentrations of AG-CDs and Al3+ are at 0.1 mg/mL and 10 μM (Fig. 2F). Simultaneously, with the addition of different concentrations of Al3+ into AG-CDs/CdTe QDs system, AG-CDs-Al3+ complex gradually forms and the emission peak for CdTe QDs gradually reduces (Fig. S12F). Then, experiments were performed to clarify the mechanism between AG-CDs-Al3+ and CdTe QDs. As shown in Fig. 2D, the average fluorescence lifetime of AG-CDs-Al3+ markedly elevates to 26.24 ns with the addition of CdTe QDs, confirming the dynamic interaction process between the excited CdTe QDs and AG-CDs-Al3+ by energy or electron transfer (Duan et al., 2024). Then, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AG-CDs-Al3+ and fluorescence spectrum of CdTe QDs were evaluated (Fig. 2G), which clearly indicates that no spectral overlap can be found. Thus, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from excited CdTe QDs to AG-CDs-Al3+ can be eliminated (Zhang et al., 2024). To verify the existence of electron transfer, energy levels of CdTe QDs were analyzed. The E g was detected and calculated as 2.85 eV for CdTe QDs based on the Tauc's plots (Fig. S13A). The redox potential was got the voltammogram (Fig. S13B), and the E LUMO and E HOMO levels for CdTe QDs were calculated as −3.96 and − 6.81 eV. As can be seen from Fig. 2H, energy bands of CdTe QDs and AG-CDs-Al3+ partially overlap, indicating the feasibility of electron transfer. CdTe QDs have higher LUMO energy than AG-CDs-Al3+, which makes the excited state electron transition from CdTe QDs to AG-CDs-Al3+ to produce PET effect, resulting in the quenched fluorescence of CdTe QDs and enhanced fluorescence of AG-CDs-Al3+.

Glyphosate with carboxylate and phosphonate groups can chelate with Al3+ (Meng et al., 2024). Hence, competitive chelation between glyphosate and AG-CDs to Al3+ can happen. Typical absorption peak for AG-CDs-Al3+ at about 410 nm disappears after introduction of glyphosate (Fig. 2I), while the average fluorescence lifetime of AG-CDs-Al3+ + glyphosate system is calculated as 18.13 ns (Fig. 2D), close to that for AG-CDs. Thus, the formation of a more stable glyphosate-Al3+ complex will release AG-CDs. Besides, glyphosate-Al3+ complex almost has no absorption peak and no fluorescent emission from 350 to 700 nm (Fig. 2I), which thus cannot affect the fluorescence of AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs probe-based detection system. Thus, the addition of glyphosate weakens the PET effect between AG-CDs-Al3+ and CdTe QDs, causing the recovery of red emission at 630 nm and the diminution of green emission at 500 nm (Scheme 1 and Fig. 2F). Encouraged by the afore-mentioned findings, a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent strategy based on AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs can be fabricated for glyphosate detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of ratiometric fluorescent sensing of glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: To improve efficiency, accuracy, and sensitivity for glyphosate detection, reaction parameters were optimized. Firstly, preparation conditions for AG-CDs were evaluated (Fig. S14). Obviously, fluorescence intensity of AG-CDs and the corresponding Al3+ response reach the maximum when ascorbic acid (0.6 g) and glycerol (10 mL, 0.137 mol) are dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL) for reaction at 160 °C for 80 min. As-prepared AG-CDs exhibit outstanding optical stability, which can be seen from Fig. S15 that the fluorescent intensities of AG-CDs remain stable in NaCl solution from 0 to 800 mM, with incubation temperatures ranging from 25 to 45 °C, storage time up to 5 months at 4 °C, under continuous 365 nm irradiation for 90 min. After that, fluorescent probe AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs were facilely fabricated by mixing AG-CDs, Al3+, and CdTe QDs (final concentrations of 0.1 mg/mL, 10 μM, and 0.05 mM) in ultrapure water (pH 7.0) and ultrasonic incubation at 25 °C for 5 min (Fig. S16,17). Subsequently, the incubation time between glyphosate and fluorescent probe was found to be 5 min (Fig. S17B). Under the optimized conditions, with the addition of glyphosate from 0 to 4 μM, the fluorescent intensity at 500 nm gradually declines while the fluorescent intensity at 630 nm enhances gradually, and a distinguishable variation of fluorescent images can be observed from green to red (Fig. 3A). As a result, the fluorescent probe AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs shows ratiometric fluorescent emission (F500/F630) characteristics with a good linear range for glyphosate concentrations from 0.2 to 4.0 μM (y = −1.3963× + 6.0618, R 2 = 0.9975) (Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 5.7 nM according to the 3σ/s method (n = 12). Comparatively, using AG-CDs-Al3+ as a single-response type, the fluorescent emission gradually decreases and the green-fluorescent images fade with the increment of glyphosate concentrations, and the LOD was determined to be 53 nM (Fig. S18), about tenfold decrease in sensitivity to ratiometric fluorescent detection. Thus, reversible signal changes in the presence of glyphosate will distinctly increase the ratio changes and then increase the sensitivity. Noticeably, Table S1 illustrates the reported metal ion-mediated fluorescent sensing strategy for glyphosate, and the fabricated dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent strategy exhibits better sensitivity.

Smartphone-assisted paper sensors are gaining popularity due to their affordability, portability, on-site accurate and sensitive detection, and user-friendly properties (Tong et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024). Here, AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs-based paper sensor has been integrated with a smartphone-based RGB-dependent analysis APP for visual analysis of glyphosate. As depicted in Fig. 3C, as the concentration of glyphosate rises, a remarkable abundant concentration-dependent color change of a green-yellow-red evolution can be observed. Afterwards, a favorable linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9951) was established for glyphosate detection with a regression equation of y = −0.1974× + 1.6344, where y is the value of G/R, x is the concentration of glyphosate (0.5–8.0 μM). The LOD was estimated to be 26 nM, which meets the requirement for the maximum residue level of glyphosate in food samples. Furthermore, the chromaticity diagram confirms the feasibility of detecting glyphosate with the naked eyes (Fig. 3D). The findings collectively establish the feasibility of on-site visual detection of glyphosate.

To assess the reliability and feasibility of AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs-based sensing platforms for the specific detection of glyphosate in practical applications, interference experiments were initially conducted. Ten pesticides were selected for evaluation, including phosphonate esters (dichlorvos, dipterex, naled), thiophosphonate esters (parathion-methyl (MP), parathion), phenyl pyrazole (fipronil), and carbamates (thiophanate-methyl (TM), carbaryl, carbendazim, thiram) (Fig. S19). As clearly demonstrated in Fig. 4A, the presence of glyphosate results in a significant decrease in the F500/F630 and G/R values, leading to the production of a red fluorescent image. In contrast, no obvious changes in the F500/F630 and G/R values are observed for other potential interfering pesticides under 10 times concentration ratio of glyphosate, resulting in a green-fluorescent image. Obviously, AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs probe can visually distinguish glyphosate from other pesticides. Furthermore, anti-interference experiments conducted in the presence of multi-pesticides reveal the negligible impact on glyphosate detection (Fig. 4B). In addition, the effects of important amino acids (DL-Alanine, Ala; L-isoleucine, L-Ile; L-phenylalanine, L-Phe; L-leucine, L-Leu; l-serine, L-Ser), metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+), and anions (SO4 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 −, CO3 2−, NO2 −) were investigated (Fig. 4C,D and S20). Results confirm that the presence of these amino acids, metal ions, and anions does not interfere with glyphosate detection. The results demonstrat that the AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs probe holds outstanding selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for the detection of glyphosate in practical samples. Furthermore, stability and repeatability were evaluated using AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs probe in solution detection and on paper sensing of glyphosate with concentrations at 0.5, 2.0, and 4.0 μM. The results indicate good precision with intra-day coefficients of variations at 1.35 % (in solution) and 2.78 % (on paper) and inter-day coefficients of variations at 3.12 % (in solution) and 5.83 % (on paper). Further, batch-to-batch coefficients of variations for CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs response to Al3+ have been found at 1.94 % (in solution) and 3.16 % (on paper) (Fig. S21).

Finally, pear, cantaloupe, and turnip samples were spiked with different amounts of glyphosate and detected by the fabricated method and high-performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS). As shown in Table 1 , no glyphosate was detected in the untreated samples, and the AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs probe-based sensing system exhibits similar spiked recovery results to those obtained by HPLC–MS method. The spiked recoveries for the AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs-based fluorescent solution platform and the paper platform are 95.0 %–105.0 % and 97.3 %–105.0 % with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.18 %–3.33 % and 1.19 %–3.33 %. The calculated Fexp (2.06–4.23) is found to be less than 5.14 at 95 % confidence (Table 1), demonstrating good consistency. These results indicate that a promising alternative for glyphosate detection in real samples has been developed with high reliability, excellent precision, and outstanding accuracy. The maximum residue level of glyphosate in fruit samples is stipulated as 0.5 mg/kg (Che et al., 2024). If 10.0 g of food samples are extracted with 10.0 mL of solvent, the maximum residue level can be calculated as 2.96 μM. As clearly shown in Fig. 3C and Table 1, a color card can be fabricated to visually discriminate safety level (green image) and dangerous level (yellow and red images) for glyphosate. Thus, this method meets the detection requirements and can be applied for point-of-care testing of food safety.

In summary, a novel dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent strategy utilizing AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs has been designed and developed for the sensitive and reliable detection of glyphosate both in laboratory settings and on-site. This strategy offers several advantages: (a) Al3+ chelation triggers the fabrication of the AG-CDs-Al3+/CdTe QDs containing CHEF and PET mechanisms with dual-reverse signals, which can distinctly amplify signal responses with greatly improved sensitivity; (b) the ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy effectively reduces errors, thereby providing more accurate and reliable sensing results; (c) the competitive chelation between glyphosate and AG-CDs for Al3+ enables the direct and rapid detection of glyphosate with high selectivity and specificity; (d) vivid changes in the color tonality of fluorescent images facilitate point-of-care visual quantification and safe/danger discrimination; (e) the use of a smartphone as a portable optical device allows for low-cost and easy-to-operate onsite results. Given that CdTe QDs are toxic, future research could involve embedding the fluorescent sensor into a hydrogel to prevent the release of CdTe QDs, thus enhancing environmental safety. In a word, this study not only develops a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric sensing platform for convenient, selective, sensitive, and portable detection of glyphosate but also provides a new insight into constructing high-performance ratiometric probes for on-site monitoring of food and environmental safety.
